items.prompt_md: What do the **read** and **write** pointers represent in a fast/slow read-write partition scan?
options: The write pointer only ever moves backward through the array
The read pointer scans every element once; the write pointer marks the next in-place slot for a kept value
Both pointers move in lockstep and are always equal to each other
The write pointer indexes into a freshly allocated output array
items.answer: The read pointer scans every element once; the write pointer marks the next in-place slot for a kept value
items.explanation_md: The read (fast) pointer inspects every element exactly once. The write (slow) pointer trails behind, marking where the next value that should survive gets written back into the *same* array — no second array involved.

items.prompt_md: What loop invariant makes a read-write partition scan correct at every step?
options: Everything left of write is already finalized/compacted; the region from write up to read is not yet decided
Everything right of read has already been discarded permanently
write and read always differ by exactly one index
The segment between write and read is always sorted in descending order
items.answer: Everything left of write is already finalized/compacted; the region from write up to read is not yet decided
items.explanation_md: That invariant is what lets the scan overwrite cells between write and read without losing information — those cells are either scratch space or values read has already consumed.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of a fast/slow read-write partition scan over an array of `n` elements?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: The read pointer visits each index once (or a small constant number of times), and the write pointer only ever moves forward — a single linear pass.

items.prompt_md: What is the extra space complexity of compacting an array in place with a read-write scan (e.g. removing duplicates from a sorted array)?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: No auxiliary array is allocated — the scan reuses the input array's own storage, needing only a couple of index variables.

items.prompt_md: Why does a single-pass read-write scan work for deduplicating a **sorted** array but not directly for an unsorted one?
options: Sorted arrays always contain fewer duplicates than unsorted ones
Sorting removes the need to track a write pointer at all
Sortedness puts every duplicate of a value adjacent to it, so comparing a candidate only to the last *written* value is enough to catch repeats
The read pointer needs random access, which only sorted data structures provide
items.answer: Sortedness puts every duplicate of a value adjacent to it, so comparing a candidate only to the last *written* value is enough to catch repeats
items.explanation_md: On unsorted data, a duplicate of an earlier value could show up anywhere later, so one comparison against the last-written value can't detect it — you'd need a set or a sort first.

items.prompt_md: In "move zeroes to the end while preserving relative order," what should happen when the read pointer lands on a non-zero value?
options: Discard it and only advance the read pointer
Write it at the write index, then advance both pointers
Immediately overwrite it with zero
Swap the read and write pointers themselves
items.answer: Write it at the write index, then advance both pointers
items.explanation_md: Every non-zero value found by read must land at the next open write slot to preserve its relative order among the other kept values; write only advances when something is actually placed.

items.prompt_md: A buggy partition scan advances the write pointer on *every* iteration, even when nothing new was written there. What's the effect?
options: The scan always terminates exactly one element too early
The array throws an index-out-of-range error immediately
Nothing — write's position only matters after the loop ends
Stale or garbage values leak into the supposedly-finalized prefix of the array
items.answer: Stale or garbage values leak into the supposedly-finalized prefix of the array
items.explanation_md: If write advances unconditionally, the region you claim is "finalized" (left of write) can contain leftover values that were never actually overwritten with a correct result — breaking the core invariant.

items.prompt_md: For "remove element" style compaction, where discarded values don't need to be preserved anywhere, why is a plain overwrite (`nums[write] = nums[read]`) usually preferred over a swap?
options: Swapping two elements always requires O(n) extra memory
Swaps are undefined behavior on arrays shorter than 2 elements
It's simpler and can't accidentally move an unexamined value into a spot the read pointer has already passed
Overwrites run in a different asymptotic time class than swaps
items.answer: It's simpler and can't accidentally move an unexamined value into a spot the read pointer has already passed
items.explanation_md: A swap would relocate whatever was sitting at write out to read's position — fine if you still need that value, but pointless extra bookkeeping when it's just being thrown away.

items.prompt_md: In the Dutch-national-flag 3-way partition (values less than / equal to / greater than a pivot), why do you swap elements instead of just overwriting them?
options: Three-way partitioning is only possible with O(n) auxiliary storage
Every element must end up somewhere in the final array — a swap relocates the displaced value instead of erasing it
The array must stay fully sorted at every intermediate step of the scan
Overwriting only works when there are exactly two output categories
items.answer: Every element must end up somewhere in the final array — a swap relocates the displaced value instead of erasing it
items.explanation_md: Unlike "remove element," nothing is being discarded here — every original value has to land in one of the three regions, so swapping (not overwriting) preserves it.

items.prompt_md: Dutch-national-flag partitioning tracks three regions with pointers (often called low, mid, high). What's its time complexity?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n) time but O(n) space
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: mid scans the array once while low and high converge inward; total pointer movement is bounded by a constant multiple of n, so it's still linear time and O(1) space.

items.prompt_md: Why is a read-write partition scan naturally stable for the elements it keeps (their original relative order is preserved)?
options: It uses recursion, and recursive calls always preserve input order
Stability isn't actually a property of this pattern — order is undefined
It sorts the array first, which guarantees stability by definition
It walks the array strictly left to right and writes survivors in the same order it encounters them
items.answer: It walks the array strictly left to right and writes survivors in the same order it encounters them
items.explanation_md: Because read only moves forward and write only ever places the *next* survivor it finds, kept elements land in the same relative order they appeared in originally.

items.prompt_md: After a read-write compaction pass removes some unwanted elements from an n-element array, what does the final value of the write pointer represent?
options: The number of elements removed
The new logical length of the compacted array
The index of the last element the read pointer visited
It's always equal to n, regardless of how many were removed
items.answer: The new logical length of the compacted array
items.explanation_md: write only advances each time a value is kept, so by the end its value equals the count of kept elements — exactly the new length callers should treat as valid.

items.prompt_md: You're given a sorted array of integers and asked to remove duplicates in place, returning the new length, using no extra array.
options: Build a brand-new array containing only the unique values
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
Hash map of counts to detect which values are duplicates
Sort the array again then rebuild it from a set
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.explanation_md: Trigger: sorted input + in-place + O(1) space requirement. A slow write pointer marks the compacted boundary while a fast read pointer scans ahead for the next distinct value — no auxiliary structure needed.

items.prompt_md: Given an integer array, move every zero to the end while keeping the relative order of the non-zero elements — modify the array in place.
options: Hash map of counts to tally zero vs non-zero elements
Sort the array by whether each element is zero
Build a new output array and copy non-zero values first, then zeros
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.explanation_md: Trigger: in-place compaction that must preserve relative order — exactly what a write pointer (next slot for a non-zero) plus a read pointer (scans ahead) is built for.

items.prompt_md: You have an array containing only the values 0, 1, and 2. Sort it in place in a single pass without using a general sorting algorithm.
options: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place (Dutch national flag, three regions)
Hash map of counts, then overwrite the array from the counts
Build a new output array and copy elements into their sorted spots
Sort then rebuild using a library sort
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place (Dutch national flag, three regions)
items.explanation_md: Trigger: fixed small set of categories, in-place, single pass — three cooperating pointers (low/mid/high) partition the array without extra space, the Dutch-flag variant of the read-write scan.

items.prompt_md: Given an array and a value, remove every occurrence of that value in place and return the new length of the array.
options: Sort then rebuild the array skipping the target value
Build a new output array excluding the target value
Hash map of counts to know how many to remove
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.explanation_md: Trigger: in-place removal, no bookkeeping about counts needed — write only advances for values that should survive; read checks every element once.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, remove duplicates in place but allow each distinct value to appear at most twice.
options: Fast/slow read+write pointers, writing a value only when it wouldn't create more than two in a row
Hash map of counts capping each value at two, then rebuild
Sort then rebuild, truncating runs longer than two
Build a new output array while counting each run's length
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers, writing a value only when it wouldn't create more than two in a row
items.explanation_md: Trigger: sorted + in-place + a bounded-repeat rule — still a write pointer, just with an extra comparison against nums[write-2] before writing.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array and a pivot value, partition it in place so smaller values come first, then equal values, then greater values — single pass, O(1) extra space.
options: Build a new output array in three sections
Hash map of counts bucketed by comparison to the pivot
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
Sort then rebuild around the pivot
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.explanation_md: Trigger: in-place, O(1) space, single pass, three ordered output regions — the same read-write scan generalized to three buckets instead of two.

items.prompt_md: Given an array, compact it in place so that only elements satisfying a given condition remain at the front, preserving their original order, with no extra memory.
options: Hash map of counts of how many elements satisfy the condition
Build a new output array of the elements that satisfy the condition
Sort by whether the condition holds, then rebuild
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
items.explanation_md: Trigger: order-preserving, in-place, O(1) space compaction — the canonical read-write scan, regardless of what the keep/discard predicate checks.

items.prompt_md: Given a sorted array, remove the duplicates in place and also report how many distinct values existed — single left-to-right pass, no extra array.
options: Sort then rebuild and count unique runs
Build a new output array and take its length
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place, using the final write index as the count
Hash map of counts, whose size is the answer
items.answer: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place, using the final write index as the count
items.explanation_md: Trigger: sorted, in-place, single pass — the final write pointer position doubles as both the compacted length and the distinct-value count, no separate counting needed.

items.prompt_md: Given an UNSORTED array, determine how many times each value occurs — you need the actual frequency of every distinct value, not just a compacted array.
options: Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
Sort then rebuild into runs and measure run lengths
Build a new output array of just the distinct values
Hash map of counts, incrementing a value's count on each occurrence
items.answer: Hash map of counts, incrementing a value's count on each occurrence
items.explanation_md: Trap: this asks for per-value frequency on unsorted data, not in-place compaction — a read-write scan only tells you what survives, never how many times something appeared. A hash map of counts answers the actual question directly in one pass.

items.prompt_md: Given an UNSORTED array, decide whether it contains any duplicate value at all — without sorting or otherwise reordering the original array.
options: Hash set membership check while scanning once, left to right
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
Sort then rebuild and scan for adjacent equal values
Build a new output array with duplicates removed, then compare lengths
items.answer: Hash set membership check while scanning once, left to right
items.explanation_md: Trap: the read-write scan's duplicate-skipping trick relies on sortedness making duplicates adjacent; here sorting (or reordering) is explicitly disallowed, and the array is unsorted to begin with — a hash set catches a repeat the moment it's seen, in original order, O(n) time.

items.prompt_md: Given an UNSORTED array and a target sum, find the original indices of the two numbers that add up to it.
options: Sort then rebuild, tracking original indices through the sort
Fast/slow read+write pointers partitioning in place
Build a new output array of every pairwise sum
Hash map from value to its index, checking for the complement as you scan
items.answer: Hash map from value to its index, checking for the complement as you scan
items.explanation_md: Trap: the array is unsorted and original indices must be preserved — sorting would scramble the indices you need to return, and there's nothing to partition/compact in place. A hash map of value-to-index finds the complement in one O(n) pass without disturbing order.
